headings.text: The Divisumma That Learned to Breathe

paragraphs.text: A study of the Olivetti Divisumma 18 — its rubber membrane, its tropical-arc housing, and the moment industrial design conceded that machines could be soft.

There is, in the lower drawer of every serious design archive, a small yellow brick that hums when you touch it. The Divisumma 18, released by Olivetti in 1969 and designed by Mario Bellini at the age of thirty-three, is not a calculator so much as a thesis on what a machine should feel like to a hand that has never operated one.

Bellini's first move was a refusal. He refused the rigid plastic keycap. He refused the chrome bezel. He refused the carriage return as a lever that announced itself with metallic violence. In their place: a continuous sheet of yellow polyurethane stretched like skin over the keyboard, dimpled into thirty-eight individual depressions, each one rising slightly above the next in a soft topographic field.

The housing — a single arc of injection-molded ABS in a chromatic key Olivetti would later call giallo Bellini — broke decisively with the rectilinear logic of the Programma 101 (Sottsass, 1965) that preceded it on the company's catalogue. Where Sottsass had drawn a confident horizontal slab, Bellini drew a wedge that leaned toward the operator, as if the object itself were performing a small bow.

This was not, as later commentators have suggested, a concession to ergonomics in the contemporary clinical sense. Ergonomics in 1969 was still, in Italy, largely a manufacturing concern — how to arrange a line, not a wrist. Bellini's wedge was rhetorical. It announced that the machine had a front and a back, a public face and a service rear.
The Divisumma 18 is the moment Italian industrial design stopped imitating the machine and started imitating the body that would touch it.— Vanni Pasca, in Domus, March 1972

The printing mechanism — a small thermal head that scrolled a forty-character paper tape across the rear platen — was hidden inside the housing in a way that no previous Olivetti calculating machine had attempted. Earlier models displayed their mechanisms as proof of their seriousness. The Divisumma 18 concealed them as proof of its sophistication. To watch the tape emerge from the rear slot was to watch a thought leave the object.

The display itself was a single line of green vacuum-fluorescent digits, recessed eleven millimeters into the housing and angled at fifteen degrees from horizontal. The recession was not decorative. It controlled glare under the overhead fluorescent lighting that was, by 1970, standard in every Milanese accounting office.

What the Divisumma 18 inaugurated, and what its descendants — the Logos 50, the Lexikon 82, eventually the Praxis 35 — would refine and ultimately exhaust, was a particular conviction: that the surface of an industrial object is not its boundary but its argument. Touch it, and you have been argued with.

By 1976 the line was discontinued. The membrane, prone to yellowing in direct sun, had begun to fade in display windows across Europe. The wedge — by then widely imitated, badly, by competitors from Casio to Sharp — had ceased to read as radical. The object retreated into the museum collection from which it has not since emerged.
"A machine that is shaped like the hand that will touch it has already conceded that it works for the hand, not against it." — Bellini, in conversation with Hans-Ulrich Obrist, 2003.
End of Object Study № 04 · continued in Issue XXXVIII as The Logos 27 Question
Nine industrial objects from the same era, selected for typological or formal proximity to the Divisumma 18.
All nine objects share a common ABS-or-polymer housing strategy. Six predate or coincide with the Divisumma 18; three are subsequent rebuttals.

items.text:
Plate II
        Keycap, numeric '7'Polyurethane dome, 14mm Ø, 3.2mm rise. Note halo of yellowing at perimeter.

Plate III
        Platen, rear printerVulcanized rubber roller with grooved index, 92mm width, in-line with paper feed.

Plate IV
        Housing, lateral arcInjection-molded ABS in giallo Bellini; single-shot mold, no visible seam.

Plate V
        Underside casingMatte black ABS, four rubber feet, service-screw access. Serial number stamped at center.

Plate VI
        Carriage leverBrushed aluminum, 38mm; the only metal exposed to the user. Cold to the touch.

Plate VII
        Imprint, brand mark"olivetti divisumma 18" set in Helvetica 8pt, debossed into the upper-right shoulder.

Plate VIII
        Rubber foot, dorsalConical, 18mm Ø; deliberately oversized to absorb impact of carriage return.

Plate IX
        Mode selector dialThree positions: NORM, MEM, PRT. Detents calibrated to give audible click at 22°.

Plate X
        Power cable, integralRubber-sheathed, non-detachable. Olivetti policy until 1974; later abandoned.

1.84kg
        Material Weight
        ABS housing, polyurethane membrane, steel chassis, lead-acid backup cell.

1969
        Year of First Production
        Released Q3 at the Salone della Meccanica, Milan; discontinued 1976.

27
        Museum Collections Holding
        Incl. MoMA NY, Triennale Milano, V&A London, Vitra Design Museum.

1963
        Joins Olivetti as chief design consultant under Roberto Olivetti.

1969
        Releases Divisumma 18 and Logos 27 in the same catalogue cycle.

1972
        Cab armchair for Cassina; the leather is the structure.

1979
        Editor-in-chief of Domus; reframes the magazine around object criticism.

1987
        Persol 649 redesign — the Bellini bridge becomes a default in eyewear.

2017
        Retrospective at the Triennale di Milano: Mario Bellini · Italian Beauty.

visibleText: Folio Obscura Quarterly · Design Criticism Cover Story Type Lab Object Study Grid Dispatch Archive Index ISSUE XXXVII · WINTER Object Study № 04 Filed: Industrial Objects / Calcolatrice Pages 042 — 058 Close Reading · Mario Bellini, 1969 № 18 The Divisumma That Learned to Breathe A study of the Olivetti Divisumma 18 — its rubber membrane, its tropical-arc housing, and the moment industrial design conceded that machines could be soft. Object Divisumma 18 Designer Mario Bellini Manufacturer Ing. C. Olivetti & C., S.p.A. Origin Ivrea, Italy Period 1969 — 1976 Plate I · Object in Plan Yellow membrane · ABS housing Photographed under raking light Fig. 0 · Olivetti Divisumma 18, electronic printing calculator. Hypothetical study photograph, three-quarter dorsal view, c. 1971. T here is, in the lower drawer of every serious design archive, a small yellow brick that hums when you touch it. The Divisumma 18, released by Olivetti in 1969 and designed by Mario Bellini at the age of thirty-three, is not a calculator so much as a thesis on what a machine should feel like to a hand that has never operated one. Bellini's first move was a refusal. He refused the rigid plastic keycap. He refused the chrome bezel. He refused the carriage return as a lever that announced itself with metallic violence. In their place: a continuous sheet of yellow polyurethane stretched like skin over the keyboard, dimpled into thirty-eight individual depressions, each one rising slightly above the next in a soft topographic field. 01 The membrane was vulcanized in a single pour. Bellini reportedly insisted on a single piece because, in his words, "a hand does not negotiate with seams." The housing — a single arc of injection-molded ABS in a chromatic key Olivetti would later call giallo Bellini — broke decisively with the rectilinear logic of the Programma 101 (Sottsass, 1965) that preceded it on the company's catalogue. Where Sottsass had drawn a confident horizontal slab, Bellini drew a wedge that leaned toward the operator, as if the object itself were performing a small bow. This was not, as later commentators have suggested, a concession to ergonomics in the contemporary clinical sense. Ergonomics in 1969 was still, in Italy, largely a manufacturing concern — how to arrange a line, not a wrist. Bellini's wedge was rhetorical. It announced that the machine had a front and a back, a public face and a service rear. 02 Compare the Programma 101's bilateral symmetry. The Divisumma 18 is the first Olivetti object that faces you . The Divisumma 18 is the moment Italian industrial design stopped imitating the machine and started imitating the body that would touch it. — Vanni Pasca, in Domus , March 1972 The printing mechanism — a small thermal head that scrolled a forty-character paper tape across the rear platen — was hidden inside the housing in a way that no previous Olivetti calculating machine had attempted. Earlier models displayed their mechanisms as proof of their seriousness. The Divisumma 18 concealed them as proof of its sophistication. To watch the tape emerge from the rear slot was to watch a thought leave the object. The display itself was a single line of green vacuum-fluorescent digits, recessed eleven millimeters into the housing and angled at fifteen degrees from horizontal. The recession was not decorative. It controlled glare under the overhead fluorescent lighting that was, by 1970, standard in every Milanese accounting office. 03 The 15° display angle was a contested decision. Olivetti's engineers wanted 22°; Bellini won by demonstrating that 15° matched the natural neck-flex of a seated bookkeeper. What the Divisumma 18 inaugurated, and what its descendants — the Logos 50, the Lexikon 82, eventually the Praxis 35 — would refine and ultimately exhaust, was a particular conviction: that the surface of an industrial object is not its boundary but its argument. Touch it, and you have been argued with. By 1976 the line was discontinued. The membrane, prone to yellowing in direct sun, had begun to fade in display windows across Europe. The wedge — by then widely imitated, badly, by competitors from Casio to Sharp — had ceased to read as radical. The object retreated into the museum collection from which it has not since emerged. "A machine that is shaped like the hand that will touch it has already conceded that it works for the hand, not against it." — Bellini, in conversation with Hans-Ulrich Obrist, 2003.
